title: Designing and Evaluating Dialogue LLMs for Co-Creative Improvised Theatre
research_questions: How can dialogue LLMs be designed and staged to perform improvisational theatre alongside human actors in real-time multi-party chat?
What technical capabilities and constraints arise when publicly available LLMs are deployed for on-the-spot multi-party improvised dialogue in a professional theatre setting?
How do audience members and performers experience AI as a co-creative actor, creative partner, and live entertainment subject?
How do human-in-the-loop interface and curation choices affect the ability of LLM-generated lines to contribute meaningfully to live improv scenes?
tasks: punny or snarky response generation for improv scenes

datasets_used.name: Edinburgh Festival Fringe 2023 Improbotics live performances
datasets_used.size: over 1750 people across 26 unique performances with various groups of 20 different improvisers

datasets_used.name: Audience surveys from Edinburgh Festival Fringe 2023 Improbotics performances
datasets_used.size: 150 unique individual responses over the course of 26 shows

datasets_used.name: Actor surveys from Edinburgh Festival Fringe 2023 Improbotics performances
datasets_used.size: 21 individual responses over the course of 26 shows

datasets_used.name: Dialogue system logs from Edinburgh Festival Fringe 2023 Improbotics performances

methods_proposed.name: Human-in-the-loop multi-party improv dialogue system
methods_proposed.summary: The system combines continuous speech recognition, an LLM server, operator-entered scene metadata, prompt buttons, and real-time curation of a stream of generated lines. Selected lines are sent via text-to-speech and an earpiece to a human “Cyborg” performer or to a robot on stage.

methods_proposed.name: Operator and Curator interfaces for live LLM theatre
methods_proposed.summary: The Operator interface lets a human type dialogue backup, scene context, character metadata, and rapid prompts such as “getting therapy,” “more snarky,” or “more punny.” The Curator interface shows generated lines from multiple LLMs without revealing which model produced them, allowing a human performer to select lines in real time.

methods_proposed.name: Participatory iterative design of AI improv game formats
methods_proposed.summary: The authors developed and revised game formats with actor feedback so that the show could test LLM capabilities while remaining entertaining. Games included Speed Dating, Wedding Speech, Couples’ Therapy, Meet the Parents, Hero’s Journey, Improvised TED Talk, and Movie Pitch.

evaluation_methods.detail: The system was deployed in a month-long live show at the 2023 Edinburgh Festival Fringe, with 26 unique performances for over 1750 people and various groups of 20 different improvisers.

Anonymous Qualtrics audience surveys collected 150 unique individual responses; all 150 answered the first 5 questions, and the subsequent 15 questions were answered on average by 109 unique individuals.
Anonymous actor surveys collected 21 individual responses over the course of 26 shows, focusing on performing with and as the Cyborg.
A human curator selected lines in real time from an anonymized stream generated by three LLMs, enabling post-performance analysis of model-line selection from the dialogue system logs.

headline_results.finding: The system was used in public performances at scale during the 2023 Edinburgh Festival Fringe.
headline_results.value: over 1750 people across 26 unique performances with various groups of 20 different improvisers
headline_results.dataset: Edinburgh Festival Fringe 2023 Improbotics live performances

headline_results.finding: The authors collected audience and actor survey data across the full run of shows.
headline_results.dataset: Audience surveys from Edinburgh Festival Fringe 2023 Improbotics performances; Actor surveys from Edinburgh Festival Fringe 2023 Improbotics performances

headline_results.finding: Audience ratings suggested the AI was perceived as relatively machine-like and weak on natural communication.
headline_results.value: naturalness of communication avg: 33; “unique mind” avg: 45; “machine-like appearance” avg: 64
headline_results.dataset: Audience surveys from Edinburgh Festival Fringe 2023 Improbotics performances

headline_results.finding: Audience ratings of the AI as an actor/improviser showed some perceived human similarity and reciprocity but high perceived ignorance of scenes.
headline_results.value: “similar to a human” avg: 53; “motivated toward mutual benefit with other actors” avg: 64; “ignorant of the scenes” avg: 76
headline_results.dataset: Audience surveys from Edinburgh Festival Fringe 2023 Improbotics performances

headline_results.finding: More respondents reported being excited about AI tools for creativity than optimistic about AI as storytellers.
headline_results.dataset: Audience surveys from Edinburgh Festival Fringe 2023 Improbotics performances

headline_results.finding: A substantial subset of respondents reported enjoying human-robot co-creation of funny stories.
headline_results.dataset: Audience surveys from Edinburgh Festival Fringe 2023 Improbotics performances

key_findings: A human-in-the-loop pipeline made it feasible to stage LLM-generated dialogue in noisy live multi-party improv, but it introduced delays and depended heavily on operator and curator skill.

After normalising by the number of generated lines, each of the three LLMs had a comparable chance of being selected by the curator, despite generating different numbers of lines because of temporary unavailability or processing speed.
Audience members were often curious and engaged by the presence of AI, but they did not perceive the AI as capable of producing consistently meaningful multi-party contextual dialogue.
Survey results suggested the show increased excitement about using AI tools for creativity more than optimism about robots as autonomous creative storytellers.

Performers enjoyed surprises, puns, wordplay, and absurd lines from A.L.Ex, but also reported challenges with delay, non-sequiturs, lack of full collaboration, and the need to justify or steer AI contributions.
The authors argue that live performance can frame AI less as a human replacement and more as an artificial co-creative subject whose limitations are visible to audiences.
limitations: The authors state that the survey results are presented for discussion rather than statistically significant claims because data were collected “in the wild” at a theatre festival with many uncontrolled variables.
Survey volume was low relative to attendance: on average, 67 audience members attended each show, but there were 5.8 survey responses per show.
The study did not collect demographic data in either actor or audience surveys in order to shorten the surveys and because of the likely high margin for error.
Speech recognition from a single stage microphone produced numerous transcription errors and did not capture physical aspects of communication such as gestures and tone of voice.
The LLMs had difficulty with turn-taking, speaker/addressee differentiation, and rapidly changing emergent social contexts in improvised scenes.

Human curation improved possible responses but created delays and did not consistently select the most appropriate lines because the curator had to listen to the stage while reading a delayed stream of generated lines.
The LLMs were not retrained on improv-specific datasets because of the late release of Llama 2 before the festival and because fine-tuning was not available for Chat GPT-3.5 and PaLM 2 at that time.
safety_ethics_notes: The study had approval from the ethics governing board at the University of Kent, collected anonymous Qualtrics surveys after consent, and included a privacy notice. The discussion raises ethical concerns about generative AI in the arts, including plagiarism, misappropriation of artistic work, copyright, generated-image appropriateness, representational biases, and devaluation of human creative work through automation.

human_samples.population: Audience members attending Improbotics AI-based improv theatre performances at the 2023 Edinburgh Festival Fringe.
human_samples.recruitment: Ticketed/public festival performances; the show framing in the festival program and on stage made clear that AI assisted the improvisers.
human_samples.observations: 26 unique performances; average attendance reported as 67 audience members per show and later as 63% seat capacity in a 106-seat venue.
human_samples.detail: This is attendance/exposure to the live system, distinct from the smaller post-performance survey sample.

human_samples.population: Audience members who attended one of the 26 shows and completed the anonymous post-performance Qualtrics survey.
human_samples.recruitment: QR code shown before and after the show; participants read a study statement and consented before proceeding.
human_samples.observations: All 150 answered the first 5 questions; the subsequent 15 questions were answered on average by 109 unique individuals; Q12 had 109 responses and Q14 had 100 responses.
human_samples.detail: No demographic data were collected; authors state the survey results are for discussion rather than statistically significant claims because data were collected in the wild with many uncontrolled variables.

human_samples.population: Improbotics human improvisers/professional actors performing with the AI system across the festival run.
human_samples.recruitment: Improbotics cast and guest improvisers; actors tried the system and provided regular feedback during participatory iterative design.
human_samples.observations: Various groups of the 20 improvisers performed across 26 shows; performer feedback and close observations informed game and interface design.
human_samples.detail: This performer population is distinct from the reported actor-survey response count.

human_samples.population: Actors/improvisers who performed with and/or as the Cyborg and completed the anonymous actor survey.
human_samples.recruitment: Survey links shared by email; participants read a study statement and consented before proceeding.
human_samples.observations: 21 individual actor-survey responses over the course of 26 shows.
human_samples.detail: The paper reports 21 individual responses, not a unique actor-participant count; no demographic data were collected.

judges.name: audience survey respondents
judges.detail: Audience respondents rated and described perceptions of A.L.Ex/the robot/AI after shows, including entertainment, anthropomorphism, responsiveness, acting ability, and attitudes toward AI creativity.

judges.name: actor survey respondents
judges.detail: Performers provided Likert-style and open-ended evaluations of performing with A.L.Ex, including meaningful contributions, collaboration, absurd/random information, story advancement, obstacles, surprise, and enjoyment.

judges.name: Curator
judges.detail: A human curator selected lines in real time from a stream of LLM-generated candidates; the paper states the person selecting lines did not know which LLM produced each response, enabling later analysis of model selection rates.

judges.name: dialogue system logs
judges.detail: Logs from all 26 performances recorded speech-recognition results, operator context metadata, LLM-generated lines, and curator-selected lines; the authors used them to analyze generated-line counts and normalized selection chances by model.

judges.name: authors/creative team close observations
judges.detail: The paper reports close observations of performances and rehearsal/design iterations, including qualitative claims about delays, non-sequiturs, turn-taking, audience laughter as an informal game-design feedback mechanism, and performer/audience experience.

generation_selection.scope: live improvised dialogue lines for A.L.Ex/Cyborg/Robot
generation_selection.n: not fixed or not reported; a continuous stream of multiple candidate lines from multiple LLMs, with prompts asking for several possible responses
generation_selection.detail: For each incoming speech-recognition or operator-context update, the system prompted LLMs and displayed generated lines; the curator selected a subset/best response in real time, without seeing which LLM produced it, and selected lines were sent via text-to-speech/earpiece or robot.

generation_selection.scope: AI-generated PowerPoint slides for Improvised TED Talk game
generation_selection.detail: The paper says this game relied on AI-generated PowerPoint slides, unbeknownst to the actor, but does not specify the generation model, number of candidates, or whether any selection/curation occurred.

generation_selection.scope: real-time generated images for Movie Pitch game
generation_selection.detail: The paper says image generators were used to output images in real time illustrating or disrupting an improvised film pitch; model names, candidate counts, and selection procedure are not reported.

model_provenance.name: gpt-3.5-turbo
model_provenance.detail: Reported as Chat GPT-3.5/OpenAI and shown as gpt-3.5-turbo in Figure 4; accessed remotely via the OpenAI ChatGPT-3.5 API. No API snapshot, exact version, or exact inference dates are reported.

model_provenance.name: text-bison
model_provenance.detail: Reported as PaLM 2/Google and shown as text-bison in Figure 4; accessed via the Google PaLM 2 API. No API snapshot or exact version is reported.

model_provenance.name: llama-2-13b-chat
model_provenance.detail: Reported as Llama 2 13B chat, served locally as a 4-bit quantized model on the stage laptop. No exact checkpoint hash or implementation version is reported.

model_provenance.name: whisper-small.en
model_provenance.detail: Used continuously for speech recognition with a USB Blue Yeti microphone; the paper reports a typical 0.3s latency on a MacBook Pro M2 with 96GB memory. No exact commit/hash is reported.

model_provenance.name: ChatGPT-4 API
model_provenance.detail: The Appendix states that the LLM server used the OpenAI ChatGPT-3.5 and ChatGPT-4 API to access remote LLMs, but the main deployment/results describe a three-LLM stream consisting of ChatGPT-3.5, PaLM 2/text-bison, and Llama 2; no GPT-4 model ID, scope of use, or results are reported.

model_provenance.name: unnamed image-generation model(s) for Movie Pitch
model_provenance.detail: The paper states that image generators were used in real time for the Movie Pitch game but does not name the model(s) or service(s).

model_provenance.name: unnamed slide-generation system for Improvised TED Talk
model_provenance.detail: The paper states that AI-generated PowerPoint slides were used for the Improvised TED Talk game but does not report the model or system details.

inference_budgets.scope: continuous speech recognition during live shows
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: continuous during the show; each line of recognised speech was sent to the language-model server
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: 4-bit quantized whisper-small.en using a modified C++ implementation on a MacBook Pro M2 with 96GB memory; typical latency reported as 0.3s; cost not reported
inference_budgets.detail: A single Blue Yeti USB microphone on a stand at the front of the stage captured speech, resulting in numerous transcription errors according to the paper.

inference_budgets.scope: dialogue LLM generation for live improvised lines
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Each incoming line of speech-recognised text and each line of operator-entered context was processed into a prompt for the LLMs; the main analysis describes three LLMs prompted simultaneously, but exact call counts are not reported
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Several possible responses were requested and displayed as a continuous stream; exact number of candidates per prompt/item is not reported
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: gpt-3.5-turbo and text-bison were remote API services; llama-2-13b-chat was 4-bit quantized and served locally on the stage laptop; API costs and rate limits are not reported
inference_budgets.detail: A typical prompt included a system prompt, speech-recognition dialogue lines prefixed by a manually entered speaker name, operator-entered context metadata, and an instruction such as “Write several possible responses for Alex.”

inference_budgets.scope: human curation and delivery of generated dialogue
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Curator viewed a stream of LLM-generated lines and selected only a small subset; exact displayed and selected counts are not reported
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Text-to-speech/radio/earpiece delivery was used, but the TTS system/model and cost are not reported
inference_budgets.detail: Selected lines were sent by text-to-speech to a human Cyborg performer’s earpiece or to a robot; the paper notes curation and delivery introduced delays.

inference_budgets.scope: AI-generated slides and images in secondary games
inference_budgets.detail: The paper mentions AI-generated PowerPoint slides and real-time image generation but does not report models, prompts, candidate counts, compute, latency, or costs.

human_baselines.scope: AI dialogue/acting ability compared with human improvisers
human_baselines.description: Human improvisers performed alongside the AI and audience questions referenced humans and robots, but the study did not include a human-only condition or matched human-generated dialogue evaluated under the same conditions.
human_baselines.detail: The paper uses human performance context and audience/actor perceptions, not a controlled directly comparable human baseline.

mitigations: Evaluation data came from live improvised 2023 performances, system logs, and post-performance surveys rather than a public legacy benchmark.
Prompts included real-time speech recognition and operator-entered scene metadata from newly improvised scenes.
contamination_risk.basis: This is a situated live-performance evaluation with newly generated dialogue context and newly collected human survey/log data, so benchmark memorization is unlikely to drive the reported audience, actor, and curation findings. The paper does not report any formal decontamination test, and model pretraining could contain general improv/comedy material, but no static public test set is central to the claims.

llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Claims are based on human curator selections, audience surveys, actor surveys, dialogue logs, and author observations; no LLM is reported as an evaluator or metric.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: LLMs generate dialogue and other creative material but do not judge, score, or label outputs in the reported evaluation.

reporting_gaps: Exact API snapshots/versions and exact inference dates are not reported for gpt-3.5-turbo, text-bison/PaLM 2, or ChatGPT-4.
The Appendix mentions ChatGPT-4 API access, but the main results describe only three LLMs; the scope and results of any GPT-4 use are unclear.
Token/context limits, sampling parameters, temperature/top-p, maximum response length, rate limits, and API costs are not reported.
Exact numbers of generated lines and curator-selected lines per model/per show are not reported; the paper only states that normalized selection chances were comparable.
The number, identity, training, and consistency of operators/curators are not fully specified; no inter-rater or multi-curator reliability is reported.
No demographic data were collected for audience or actor survey respondents.

Survey response attrition is substantial: all 150 audience respondents answered the first five questions, but later questions averaged 109 unique individuals; the paper presents results for discussion rather than statistical significance.
No controlled human baseline or human-only comparison condition is reported.
The text-to-speech system/model is not named.
Image-generation and slide-generation models used in secondary games are not named, and their prompts/budgets/selection procedures are not reported.
Audience laughter is described as an informal feedback/success criterion for game design but is not quantitatively measured.
Raw survey data, dialogue logs, model outputs, and code are not reported as publicly released in the paper.
